basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Burūj — “The Constellations” — opens with three oaths — the constellated heaven, the promised Day, the witness and the witnessed — to evoke the story of the aṣḥāb al-ukhdūd, the People of the Trench: those believers thrown alive into the fire by a tyrant king because they refused to abjure. Tradition most often identifies them with the Christians of Najrān, massacred in the 6th century by Dhū Nuwās, king of Yemen. The sūra devotes four verses of icy soberness to them: they reproached them nothing except that they had believed. At the heart of the sūra, Qushayrī lingers on the pair Shāhid wa Mashhūd (witness and witnessed) — and gives ten readings rising by stages to the highest: The Witness is God Himself, witnessing Himself by Unicity; and the Witnessed is also He — because He witnessed Himself. The sūra closes on the image of the Lawḥ maḥfūẓ, the Preserved Tablet where the Quran is kept. Qushayrī adds, in full light: just as the Quran is kept in the Tablet, it is kept in the hearts of believers. The Tablet and the heart, two places of the same preservation.

versets.trad: By the heaven adorned with constellations!
versets.commentaire: al-burūj (البُرُوج): Qushayrī specifies simply: “He means the twelve zodiacal constellations” — Ram, Bull, Twins, Crab, Lion, Virgin, Scales, Scorpion, Archer, Goat, Water-bearer, Fishes. The heaven is named not for its emptiness but for its ornament: the very regularity of its constellations is the sign of an order that thinks.

mots.def: burūj: plural of burj; towers, constellations; here, the twelve signs of the zodiac.

versets.trad: By the promised Day!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī, in two words: “the Day of Resurrection.” The word mawʿūd (مَوْعُود) — promised — is of the root w-ʿ-d, which names the divine promise. That Day is not a contingency; it is a promise kept, whose horizon shapes the whole of present life.

mots.def: mawʿūd: passive participle of waʿada; promised.

versets.trad: By the witness and by the witnessed!
versets.commentaire: An enigmatic verse upon which Qushayrī gives ten readings tracing a whole metaphysics of witness. (1) “The Witness is God; the Witnessed is creation.” (2) An inverted and more beautiful reading: “The Witness is creation; the Witnessed is God — creatures contemplate Him today with their hearts, and tomorrow with their eyes.” (3) “The Witness is Muḥammad; the Witnessed is the Resurrection.” (4) “The Witness is Friday; the Witnessed is the Day of ʿArafa.” (5) “The Witness is the angel who writes the deed; the Witness is also man who testifies against himself — and his limbs testify against him. He is at once witness and witnessed.” (6) “The Witness is the community of Muḥammad; the Witnessed are the other communities.” (7) “The Witness is the Black Stone — for it carries the writing of the Covenant.” (8) “The Witness is the whole of creation, witnessing to God's Unicity; the Witnessed is God.” And the highest reading, given by Qushayrī last: (9-10) “The Witness is God: He has witnessed Himself the Unicity; and the Witnessed is He — because He has witnessed Himself.” The most inward testimony is the one in which God, witness and witnessed, closes upon Himself the loop of Unicity. All the depth of the verse lies in this distance: from the witness the creature bears about God, to the witness God bears about Himself.

mots.def: shāhid: active participle of shahida; witness, one who beholds.
mashhūd: passive participle of shahida; that which is borne witness to, contemplated.

versets.trad: Cursed be the People of the Trench!
versets.commentaire: qutila (قُتِلَ): Qushayrī: “they have been cursed.” The passive form serves here to utter an imprecation, as we would say “cursed be...” al-ukhdūd (الأُخْدُود): the long trench dug in the ground. Tradition reports several identifications (people of Sijistān, of Najrān, of Constantinople, Magi, Jews, Christians). The identification most accepted by modern history points to the Christians of Najrān, massacred at the beginning of the 6th century by Dhū Nuwās, the Jewish king of Yemen, who had them thrown into burning trenches for refusing to abjure their faith.

mots.def: qatala in the passive perfect; here as imprecation.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ukhdūd: an elongated trench dug in the ground.

versets.trad: Their fire was ceaselessly fed,
versets.commentaire: al-waqūd (الوَقُود): Qushayrī, in a word: “firewood.” The image is not of a spontaneous fire: it is a fire maintained, fuelled by the hand of the torturers. The cruelty lies in that the torment is not an accident — it is methodical work.

versets.trad: while they sat at its edge,
versets.commentaire: quʿūd: seated. A terrible detail. The torturers do not kill in haste: they sit down at the edge of the trench to watch. Evil is no longer only active; it is spectator. Qushayrī reports the tradition: “While the king's companions were seated around him, watching what was happening, the fire rose from the trench and burnt all who were watching — while the believers in the fire came out safely.”

mots.def: preposition + feminine suffix pronoun (referring to the trench).

versets.ar: وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَفْعَلُونَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ
versets.tr: wa-hum ʿalā mā yafʿalūna bi-l-muʾminīna shuhūd
versets.trad: watching what they inflicted upon the believers.
versets.commentaire: shuhūd: witnesses, present. And here, in dark mirror of verse 3, the reverse of witness: bearing witness without pity to what one inflicts. The sūra plays on the same word — shāhid / shuhūd — to distinguish the witness that saves and the one that condemns. Tradition reports the final detail: the last to enter the fire was a mother carrying her nursing child; she hesitated. The child spoke and said to her: “Stand firm and patient; you are in the truth.” She went in. When the fire rose from the trench, it was the seated torturers it consumed — the believers came out safe.

mots.def: muʾminīn: plural of muʾmin; the believers.
mots.gram: preposition + plural active participle, definite genitive

mots.def: shuhūd: plural of shāhid; witnesses, present.

versets.ar: وَمَا نَقَمُوا مِنْهُمْ إِلَّا أَن يُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ
versets.tr: wa-mā naqamū minhum illā an yuʾminū bi-Llāhi l-ʿazīzi l-ḥamīd
versets.trad: They reproached them nothing except that they had believed in God, the Almighty, the Praiseworthy,
versets.commentaire: A verse chilling in its simplicity. naqamū: they blamed, they took offence. Qushayrī: “they grew angry with them only for their faith.” Absolute evil named in eight words: to persecute someone precisely because he believes. And the verse accentuates by the two Names — al-ʿAzīz al-Ḥamīd, the Almighty, the Praiseworthy — whom no persecution diminishes.

mots.def: restrictive negation particle paired with illā.
naqama: to blame, to take offence.
ʿAzīz: Divine Name; the Almighty, the Inaccessible.
Ḥamīd: Divine Name; He who deserves all praise.

versets.ar: الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۚ وَاللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ
versets.tr: alladhī lahu mulku s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-Llāhu ʿalā kulli shayʾin shahīd
versets.trad: to whom belongs the Royalty of the heavens and the earth. And God is witness over every thing!
versets.commentaire: The verse loops back to the central theme: God is shahīd over every thing. The word returns — it had named the torturers shuhūd of the evil they were doing. They bore witness seated, satisfied. He bears witness, and there is no escape from His witnessing.

mots.def: samāwāt: plural of samāʾ; the heavens.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَتُوبُوا فَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ الْحَرِيقِ
versets.tr: inna lladhīna fatanū l-muʾminīna wa-l-muʾmināti thumma lam yatūbū fa-lahum ʿadhābu jahannama wa-lahum ʿadhābu l-ḥarīq
versets.trad: Truly, those who have tormented the believing men and women and then have not repented shall suffer the chastisement of Hell, the burning chastisement.
versets.commentaire: fatanū (فَتَنُوا): they burned, tried by fire, made suffer. Qushayrī: “they burned the believing men and women, and did not repent of their denial.” A capital detail: the sūra leaves the door of repentance open. The verse only closes after the expression thumma lam yatūbū — “then did not repent.” Even for those who did this, the door of return is not barred. But it requires crossing.

mots.def: fatana: to test by fire, to torment, to persecute.

mots.gram: feminine plural active participle, definite accusative
apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْكَبِيرُ
versets.tr: inna lladhīna āmanū wa-ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāti lahum jannātun tajrī min taḥtihā l-anhāru dhālika l-fawzu l-kabīr
versets.trad: Truly, those who have believed and done righteous deeds shall have Gardens beneath which the rivers flow. That is the perfect happiness!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī, soberly: “al-fawz al-kabīr, that is the great salvation.” Against the horizon of the Fire described in the preceding verse, this verse places its absolute opposite: Gardens watered by rivers. The sūra thus draws two symmetrical destinies, one built on persecution, the other on faith held fast.

versets.trad: Truly, the grip of your Lord is severe.
versets.commentaire: baṭsh (بَطْش) — Qushayrī: “the seizing with force.” It is the firmness that does not let go. The verse is the very response of the oath — for which the constellated heaven, the promised Day, the Witness and the Witnessed, and the story of the Trench had sworn. The lesson is simple: do not let yourself be deceived by the apparent delay. The grip of God comes.

mots.def: baṭsh: firm grip, seizing with force.

versets.trad: It is He who originates the creation and renews it.
versets.commentaire: A verse that Qushayrī unfolds in five successive readings. (1) “He begins the creation, then returns it after the resurrection.” (2) “He begins with chastisement, then renews it; and with reward, then renews it.” (3) “He begins by setting enmity and wretchedness in someone, then returns it upon him. He begins with weakness, and brings them back to weakness.” Then the two most beautiful readings: (4) “He makes the luminous states appear; then, when a veil falls, He makes them appear again.” (5) “He sends forth the subtleties of His self-disclosure (laṭāʾif al-taʿrīf, لَطَائِف التَّعْرِيف), then He sends them back so that those lights remain forever visible — and He never ceases to send forth and bring back until the end of life.” The image is splendid: the whole spiritual life as a pulsation between light appeared and light given again. Nothing is ever fixed; all is ceaselessly resumed.

mots.def: abdaʾa: to begin, to bring forth.
aʿāda: to make return, to recommence.

versets.trad: He is the Forgiver, the Loving,
versets.commentaire: An overwhelming verse. al-Ghafūr (الغَفُور): “He who forgives abundantly.” al-Wadūd (الوَدُود): Qushayrī specifies, “intensive form of al-wādd, He who loves. And it also means He who is loved (al-mawdūd).” Then the full sentence, magnificent: “He forgives them much because He loves them; and He forgives them much because they love Him.” Forgiveness is not a calculus of justice: it is the circulation of a love that goes both ways. And the word wadūd, by its double active/passive valence, says that God's love is, at once, what He gives and what He receives.

mots.def: Ghafūr: intensive form of ghāfir; He who forgives abundantly. Divine Name.
Wadūd: intensive form; He who loves ardently, and also He who is loved. Divine Name.

versets.trad: Master of the Throne, the Magnanimous,
versets.commentaire: al-ʿarsh (العَرْش): the Throne — Qushayrī: “the elevated kingdom.” al-majīd (المَجِيد): “the noble glory.” The root m-j-d names glory in the sense of majestic radiance, magnanimity that shines by its own nobility. Forgiveness and love come from this very majesty.

versets.trad: He who realises all that He wills.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “because He is the absolute Master, nothing restricts Him, nothing is forbidden Him.” faʿʿāl is an intensive form: not only does He act, He never ceases acting. The divine will unfolds without obstacle. And one hears, behind it, the counter-image of the tyrant king of the preceding verses: he believed he could do anything; He alone truly can.

mots.def: faʿʿāl: intensive form of fāʿil; who does abundantly, who acts ceaselessly.

versets.trad: Has the report of the armies reached you,
versets.commentaire: al-junūd (الجُنُود): Qushayrī: “the masses of the disbelievers.” The sūra will now name two famous examples — Pharaoh and Thamūd — to recall that persecution of believers by the powerful is not new, and that the outcome has not changed.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: junūd: plural of jund; armies, troops.

versets.trad: of Pharaoh and Thamūd?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “their case has already been mentioned” — alluding to sūras 89 and 91. Pharaoh, the political tyrant; Thamūd, the people who swelled. Both were powerful; both vanished. The sūra names them briefly, knowing the reader already remembers.

versets.trad: Yet, the disbelievers persist in crying lies,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “those who have disbelieved — the Meccan polytheists — are in denial of Resurrection and Gathering.” Despite the historical examples, they continue. That is what makes the warning so solemn: the past does not suffice to enlighten those who blind themselves.

mots.def: takdhīb: verbal noun of kadhdhaba; the act of holding as a lie.

versets.trad: while God holds them at His mercy.
versets.commentaire: muḥīṭ (مُحِيط): Qushayrī, in one word: “knowing them.” The word has a strong spatial charge: He who encompasses. God is not in front; He encompasses. The verse states the paradox: those who deny Him live inside His knowledge without even seeing it. Like a fish that would deny the ocean.

mots.def: warāʾ: behind, beyond; with suffix pronoun.
muḥīṭ: active participle of aḥāṭa; who surrounds, who encompasses.

versets.trad: This is indeed a glorious Quran,
versets.commentaire: qurʾān majīd: a glorious Quran. The same word majīd as in verse 15 (God, Master of the Throne, the Glorious). The Book bears the glory of Him who sent it down. The sūra announces what follows — what comes is neither lie nor accident: it is the Word borne by His glory.

versets.trad: written on a Preserved Tablet!
versets.commentaire: al-Lawḥ al-maḥfūẓ (اللَّوْح المَحْفُوظ) — the Preserved Tablet. The tradition reported by Qushayrī: “The Preserved Tablet was created from a white pearl, and its two faces are of red ruby; its breadth is between heaven and earth, its top is attached to the Throne, and its base rests in the lap of a noble angel.” But Qushayrī closes the sūra with a wonderful note: “Just as the Quran is kept in the Tablet, it is kept also in the hearts of believers — as God says (29:49): ‘Nay, they are clear signs in the breasts of those who have been given knowledge.' It is therefore written in the Tablet, and preserved in the hearts.” The Tablet and the heart: two places of one same Word.

mots.def: maḥfūẓ: passive participle of ḥafiẓa; preserved, kept.
